sources_exist.note: All 12 cited URL occurrences resolved to accessible pages or the IRS PDF, although resolution alone does not establish the quoted claims.

figures_match_sources.note: The IRS analog, Auxo buy range, CT Acquisitions platform range, CPA Trendlines deal activity, and several qualitative claims are findable, but inputs.current_adoption_pct.url does not contain the quoted Wolters Kluwer 9%-to-41% figure; inputs.owners_60plus_pct.url supports approximately 25% overall but not the quoted above-40% small-firm or 65%-over-50 claims; inputs.pricing_structure.url does not contain the quoted 79% bookkeeping statistic; and inputs.active_consolidators.url does not directly establish 30 distinct operating platforms doing add-ons.

judgment_inputs_plausible.note: Role fractions are bounded and concrete and t50=3 years is within its declared range, but pi_dist=0.55 is not reproducibly blended by attest/tax/CAS revenue share as the prompt requires and relies on an unsupported 79% pricing-share statistic. The owner-age input of 0.33 is also an unshown extrapolation from a source that reports about 25% overall.

rubric_consistent.note: The formulas and deterministic dataset inputs follow the frozen rubric, and the assigned borderline flag is arithmetically consistent because S=5.372612028417493 is 0.1274 below the 5.50 strong cut. However, the B owner input and bonus, A derivation, and C capture input depend on unsupported or incompletely sourced research, so the rubric cannot be considered consistently evidenced.

cross_checks_honest.note: All four mandatory cross-checks are present. Uplift is consistent, the terminal discussion separates attest, tax, and CAS, no-proxy use is explicitly confirmed, and pricing deflation is acknowledged in pi_dist rather than hidden.

source_audits.note: The page documents heavy recent activity, 52 distinct PE sponsors since 2024, and named repeat acquirers, but it does not count 30 distinct PE-backed operating platforms actively doing add-ons in the last 24 months; sponsors and operating platforms are not interchangeable.
The guide defines normalized EBITDA after partner/replacement-labor adjustments and gives traditional local CPA practices a 3.0x-5.0x normalized EBITDA range, supporting the selected 4.0x midpoint.

The article supports 21% of tax firms already using GenAI, 53% planning or considering it, and 25% with no plans. It does not contain the additionally quoted Wolters Kluwer 9% in 2024 to 41% in 2025 statistic, yet that figure is used in the t50 derivation.
The accessible guide states a 10.0x-15.0x adjusted-EBITDA range for platform-tier targets above $10M EBITDA in the 2025-Q2 2026 window, which supports selecting 11.0x within that range.
The IRS history states nationwide expansion in 1990 and that e-filed returns crossed 50% in 2005 with 68.4 million returns, supporting the approximately 15-year e-file analog.

The linked CPA Journal article states that approximately 25% of partners are older than 60. Its accessible text does not support the record's quoted above-40% small-firm share, the 65%-over-50 claim, or the resulting owner-weighted 33% estimate.

Karbon describes hourly, fixed-fee, value-based, and subscription models and explicitly explains that hourly billing does not reward automation efficiency, supporting the qualitative pricing-deflation mechanism. It does not provide the separate 79% statistic claimed elsewhere.

The URL redirects to the 2026 report, which presents standard-rate, realization, and net-realized-rate comparisons and supports the claim that rate discipline can preserve pricing power despite modest realization differences.
The article reports shrinking talent pools and hiring/retention as the top industry issue, supporting the labor-scarcity counterweight invoked in the pi_dist rationale.

The page explains that the licensed attest entity performs audit, review, and compilation work and remains majority CPA-owned, supporting the regulatory ownership barrier and APS distinction used in the moat rationale.
The article supports an aging partner base and succession pressure, which are relevant to the stated supply barrier, though it does not quantify generic-AI bypass risk.

The guide describes hourly, fixed, value-based, and subscription pricing and quotes roughly $200-$400 hourly rates, but it does not contain the record's exact claim that 79% of firms moved away from hourly billing for bookkeeping or establish revenue shares by pricing model.

reasons: Re-run inputs.current_adoption_pct and inputs.t50_years with a direct URL for the Wolters Kluwer 9%-to-41% adoption statistic, or remove that unsupported figure from the derivation; https://www.resultsense.com/news/2025-11-06-accounting-firms-ai-adoption-big-4-small-firms/ supports 21% current tax-firm use but not the quoted Wolters Kluwer series.

Re-research inputs.owners_60plus_pct.value and its succession chain using a source that directly supports the small-firm age distribution and the 33% blend; https://www.cpajournal.com/2024/01/23/state-of-the-profession-3/ supports approximately 25% overall but not the quoted above-40% and 65% figures.

Replace or substantiate inputs.pricing_structure.figure_quoted and recompute the evidence basis for inputs.pi_dist with actual attest/tax/CAS revenue weights; https://karbonhq.com/resources/accounting-services-pricing-guide/ describes pricing models but does not report the claimed 79% bookkeeping share.

Substantiate inputs.active_consolidators.value with a source or documented enumeration of distinct PE-backed operating platforms that completed add-ons in the last 24 months; https://cpatrendlines.com/2026/02/16/cornerstone-pe-deal-tracker-pe-update-alan-whitman-plants-a-flag-in-the-private-equity-landscape-2020-2026/ reports sponsors and transactions but not the claimed count of 30 operating platforms.
